Keerthana Singareddy ← All Case Studies
Product Design User Experience Transparency

Improving Fare Transparency in Rapido to Reduce Ride Fare Disputes

Redesigning the waiting charges experience to improve transparency, reduce fare disputes, and increase user trust.

The Problem

Rapido currently adds waiting charges only after the ride ends, leading to unexpected fare increases and user frustration. Users often experience surprise when they see the final bill, as waiting charges accumulate silently during the ride without any real-time visibility. This lack of transparency creates a trust gap between riders and the platform and frequently results in disputes with drivers.

Core Question: Why doesn't Rapido show waiting charges in real-time during the ride, and how can we improve transparency without disrupting the user experience?

Evidence of the Problem

Possible signals indicating this is a real issue:

  • App store complaints: Reviews mentioning "unexpected charges" and "hidden fees"
  • Customer support tickets: High volume of fare-related disputes and refund requests
  • Driver feedback: Reports of frequent rider arguments over waiting time accuracy
  • Cancellation patterns: Users canceling rides after seeing final fare
  • Trust metrics: Lower NPS scores related to pricing transparency

Understanding the Current State

Current User Journey

  1. User books a ride and sees estimated fare
  2. Driver arrives and waits (charges start accumulating silently)
  3. Ride starts - user has no visibility into waiting charges
  4. Ride ends - user sees final fare with waiting charges added
  5. User is surprised by the higher amount

Possible Reasons Behind Current Design

Hypotheses for why the current implementation exists:

  • Simplified billing architecture: Calculating final charges at the end is technically simpler than real-time updates
  • Reducing ride-time distractions: Avoiding interruptions to the user during the journey
  • Legacy fare calculation systems: The billing system may not support real-time charge updates
  • Accurate waiting time validation: Ensuring drivers get compensated for actual waiting time
  • Minimizing development complexity: Real-time updates require WebSocket infrastructure

User Pain Points

  • Unexpected fare increases lead to negative surprises
  • No way to track or control waiting charges during the ride
  • Disputes with drivers about waiting time accuracy
  • Loss of trust in the platform's pricing transparency
  • Users feel "tricked" when they see the final bill

Research & Discovery

Research Approach

To validate this problem and understand user needs, I would employ the following research methods:

  • App store review analysis: Mining 1000+ reviews for patterns related to fare transparency
  • User interviews: Conducting 20-30 interviews with frequent Rapido riders
  • Driver interviews: Understanding driver perspective on fare disputes
  • Support ticket analysis: Analyzing 6 months of fare-related complaints
  • Competitive product analysis: Evaluating how Uber, Ola, and Lyft handle waiting charges
  • User journey mapping: Identifying pain points in the current booking-to-payment flow

Competitive Analysis

  • Uber: Shows real-time fare updates during the ride with clear breakdowns
  • Ola: Displays waiting time and charges before ride starts with countdown timer
  • Lyft: Provides fare breakdown with waiting time included in upfront estimate

Key Takeaway: Competitors prioritize transparency in fare breakdowns, which helps reduce rider confusion and disputes. Rapido currently has an opportunity to differentiate through proactive, real-time transparency that builds trust before issues arise.

Key Insights

  • Users value transparency over simplicity - they want to know what they're paying for
  • Real-time visibility reduces post-ride disputes by setting clear expectations
  • Clear communication builds trust and increases platform loyalty
  • Users want control over their spending and the ability to make informed decisions
  • Drivers benefit from transparency as it reduces confrontational situations

Problem Framing

User Story

"As a Rapido user, I want to see waiting charges accumulating in real-time so that I can understand my final fare and avoid surprises at the end of my ride."

Success Metrics

-40%
Fare Dispute Rate
+25%
User Trust Score (NPS)
-30%
Support Tickets
+15%
Repeat Usage Rate

Business Impact Metrics

Additional metrics to track business health:

  • Driver satisfaction: +10% improvement in driver retention
  • Refund rate: -25% reduction in fare-related refunds
  • Ride completion rate: +5% fewer cancellations post-booking
  • Customer lifetime value: +12% increase due to improved trust
  • Support cost: -35% reduction in fare dispute handling time

Constraints

  • Must not distract users during the ride
  • Should work with existing billing infrastructure
  • Needs to be clear for both users and drivers
  • Must handle edge cases (network issues, GPS errors)

Proposed Solution

Solution Prioritization

I evaluated potential solutions based on:

  • Implementation complexity: Engineering effort and technical feasibility
  • User impact: Expected improvement in user experience and trust
  • Engineering effort: Development time and resource requirements
  • Business value: Potential reduction in disputes and support costs

Recommended Three-Pronged Approach

Priority Order:

1️⃣ Pre-Ride Transparency (High Impact, Low Effort)

  • Show waiting charge policy clearly during booking
  • Display "Free waiting time: 2 minutes" prominently
  • Explain charges: "₹1/minute after 2 minutes"
  • Set expectations before the ride starts
  • Why first: Quick win, no real-time infrastructure needed

2️⃣ Real-Time Visibility (High Impact, Medium Effort)

  • Add a subtle "Waiting charges" indicator on the ride screen
  • Show live timer: "Waiting: 3 min (₹3)"
  • Update fare estimate in real-time
  • Non-intrusive but always visible
  • Why second: Core solution, requires backend work

3️⃣ Post-Ride Clarity (Medium Impact, Low Effort)

  • Detailed fare breakdown with waiting time highlighted
  • Timeline showing when waiting charges were applied
  • Option to dispute with one tap
  • Educational content about waiting charges
  • Why third: Reduces disputes after they occur, not preventive

Design Solutions

Below are the key screens showing the improved waiting charges experience:

1. Booking Screen

Clear waiting policy shown upfront with estimated charges

2. Driver Waiting Alert

Notification when driver arrives with countdown timer

3. Live Ride Screen

Real-time waiting charges displayed subtly during ride

4. Fare Breakdown

Detailed breakdown with waiting time timeline

Key Design Decisions

  • Subtle but visible: Charges shown in a non-intrusive card that doesn't block the map
  • Color coding: Green for free time, yellow for approaching limit, red for charges
  • Progressive disclosure: Tap to see detailed breakdown without leaving the screen
  • Contextual help: "?" icon explains waiting charges policy

Implementation Approach

Experimentation Strategy

The rollout would begin with an A/B test comparing the current experience with the new real-time transparency design:

  • Control Group (50%): Current experience (charges shown only at end)
  • Test Group (50%): New experience with real-time visibility
  • Duration: 2 weeks minimum for statistical significance
  • Sample Size: 10,000+ rides per group

Phase 1: Foundation (Week 1-2)

  • Update booking screen with waiting policy
  • Add waiting time tracking to backend
  • Create real-time charge calculation API
  • Set up A/B testing infrastructure

Phase 2: Real-Time Display (Week 3-4)

  • Implement live waiting charges UI
  • Add WebSocket for real-time updates (or polling fallback)
  • Test with internal users and beta testers
  • Monitor performance and battery impact

Phase 3: Controlled Rollout (Week 5-6)

  • Launch A/B test with 10% of users in test group
  • Monitor key metrics daily (disputes, NPS, cancellations)
  • Gather qualitative feedback through in-app surveys
  • Iterate based on data and user feedback

Phase 4: Full Rollout (Week 7-8)

  • If metrics improve by 20%+, expand to 50% of users
  • Continue monitoring for edge cases
  • Full rollout if no major issues detected
  • Document learnings and best practices

Technical Considerations

  • Real-time sync: Use WebSockets or polling for live updates
  • Offline handling: Cache charges locally and sync when online
  • GPS accuracy: Handle cases where location data is unreliable
  • Performance: Ensure updates don't drain battery or data
  • Scalability: Design for millions of concurrent rides

Expected Impact

User Benefits

  • No more surprise charges at the end of rides
  • Better understanding of fare components
  • Ability to make informed decisions (e.g., hurry to reduce waiting time)
  • Increased trust in the platform

Business Benefits

  • Reduced customer support load
  • Fewer fare disputes and refunds
  • Improved user retention
  • Better driver satisfaction (less conflict)
  • Competitive advantage through transparency

Projected Metrics

40%
Reduction in Disputes
25%
Increase in Trust Score
30%
Fewer Support Tickets
15%
Higher Retention

Risks & Mitigation

Potential Risks

  • User anxiety: Seeing charges accumulate might stress users
    Mitigation: Use calming colors (green for free time) and clear messaging about control
    Impact: Low - transparency reduces anxiety more than it creates it
  • Technical complexity: Real-time updates require robust infrastructure
    Mitigation: Start with polling, move to WebSockets gradually
    Impact: Medium - manageable with phased rollout
  • Driver pushback: Drivers might worry about users canceling
    Mitigation: Educate drivers on long-term benefits of transparency
    Impact: Low - drivers benefit from fewer disputes
  • Increased cancellations: Users might cancel when they see charges accumulating
    Mitigation: This is actually good - prevents disputes later
    Impact: Short-term revenue dip, long-term trust gain
    Business Risk: 5-10% increase in cancellations during waiting period
  • Revenue impact: Short-term revenue drop from cancellations
    Mitigation: Offset by increased repeat usage and reduced refunds
    Impact: Net positive after 3 months based on competitor data

Success Criteria for Rollout

Proceed with full rollout if:

  • Fare dispute rate decreases by at least 20%
  • User trust score (NPS) increases by at least 10%
  • Support ticket volume decreases by at least 15%
  • Cancellation rate increase is less than 10%
  • No significant technical issues or crashes

What I'd Do Differently With Real Data

If I had access to Rapido's actual data and resources, I would:

Data Analysis

  • Waiting time distribution: Analyze patterns - what percentage of rides have waiting charges? What's the average waiting time?
  • Fare dispute frequency: Quantify how many disputes are specifically related to waiting charges vs other fare components
  • Cancellation patterns: Identify if users cancel more often when waiting time exceeds certain thresholds
  • Driver wait patterns: Understand driver behavior - are they arriving early? How long do they typically wait?
  • Geographic patterns: Are certain areas more prone to waiting charges? (traffic, parking issues)
  • Time-based patterns: Do waiting charges occur more during peak hours or specific times of day?

User Segmentation

  • Frequent riders: Do power users understand waiting charges better?
  • New users: Are first-time riders more likely to dispute waiting charges?
  • Price-sensitive users: How do different user segments react to waiting charges?
  • Corporate vs personal: Different tolerance levels for waiting charges

Competitive Benchmarking

  • Conduct user testing with Uber/Ola's waiting charge experience
  • Analyze competitor app store reviews for waiting charge complaints
  • Compare dispute rates across platforms (if data available)
  • Understand best practices from other markets (US, Southeast Asia)

Experimentation

  • Multivariate testing: Test different UI placements for waiting charge indicator
  • Message testing: A/B test different ways of communicating waiting charges
  • Threshold testing: Experiment with different free waiting time durations (1 min vs 2 min vs 3 min)
  • Color psychology: Test different color schemes for charge indicators

Qualitative Research

  • Conduct 30+ in-depth user interviews about fare transparency
  • Run usability tests with interactive prototypes
  • Shadow riders during actual trips to observe behavior
  • Interview drivers about their perspective on waiting charges
  • Analyze support call recordings for common pain points

Business Impact Modeling

  • Calculate current cost of fare disputes (refunds + support time)
  • Model potential revenue impact of increased cancellations
  • Estimate customer lifetime value improvement from increased trust
  • Project driver retention impact from reduced conflicts

Key Learnings

  • Transparency builds trust: Users prefer knowing costs upfront, even if higher
  • Real-time feedback matters: Immediate visibility prevents negative surprises
  • Design for clarity: Complex information needs simple, intuitive presentation
  • Balance is key: Show important info without overwhelming users
  • Test assumptions: A/B testing is crucial for validating product decisions
  • Think marketplace: Solutions must work for both riders and drivers
  • Data-driven decisions: Real user data would significantly improve solution accuracy

Alternative Approaches Considered

Option 1: Push Notifications

Send notifications when waiting charges start accumulating.
Rejected because: Too intrusive and users might miss them.

Option 2: Post-Ride Only Improvements

Just improve the fare breakdown screen without real-time updates.
Rejected because: Doesn't solve the core transparency problem.

Option 3: Opt-in Real-Time Updates

Let users choose whether to see live charges.
Rejected because: Adds complexity and most users would want this by default.

Next Steps

  1. Conduct user research to validate assumptions
  2. Create high-fidelity prototypes in Figma
  3. Run usability testing with 20-30 users
  4. Build MVP with basic real-time display
  5. A/B test with 10% of users for 2 weeks
  6. Analyze data and iterate
  7. Full rollout if metrics improve by 20%+
